leverage-voxkit.title: Leverage VoxKit for your research
leverage-voxkit.description: Comprehensive guide to VoxKit's Pipeline Stackers for speech processing workflows

sections.content: VoxKit's Pipeline interface is built around Stackers—specialized workflow pages that guide you through multi-step speech processing tasks. This guide covers Engines, Analyzers, and Stackers that power your research workflows.
Stackers are the main workflow pages in VoxKit's Pipeline interface. Each stacker uses numbered steps (①②③④⑤⑥) to guide users through specific speech processing tasks in a logical sequence.
VoxKit provides four main stackers for different speech processing workflows:
Train or fine-tune alignment models using existing datasets and alignments.

items: ① Engine Radio Buttons: Select alignment method (MFA, W2TG, etc.) with base model dropdown
② Base Model Dropdown: Choose which pre-trained model to fine-tune from (embedded in ① panel)
③ Dataset Dropdown: Select registered training dataset containing audio files
④ Alignment Dropdown: Choose alignment output to use as training TextGrids (auto-populated based on ③)
⑤ Model Name Input: Enter unique name for the new trained model
⑥ Start Training Button: Execute model training with selected configuration

sections.content: Key Features: Dynamic engine selection with exclusive radio buttons, base model dropdown per engine (shows/hides based on selection), alignment selection depends on chosen dataset, and settings accessible via gear icon (⚙️).
Generate forced alignments for datasets using trained models.

items: ① Engine Radio Buttons: Select alignment method (MFA, W2TG, etc.)
② Model Dropdown: Choose trained model for alignment (embedded in ① panel, per-engine)
③ Dataset Dropdown: Select registered dataset to align
④ Start Predicting Button: Execute alignment prediction on dataset

sections.content: Key Features: Similar engine selection pattern to TrainingStacker, each engine has its own model dropdown (visibility toggled), settings accessible via gear icon (⚙️), and no manual path inputs required.
Extract Goodness of Pronunciation (GOP) scores using phoneme log-likelihood ratios.

items: ① Dataset Dropdown: Select dataset containing speech data for PLLR extraction
② Alignment Dropdown: Choose alignment output to use for phoneme boundaries (auto-populated based on ①)
③ Output Path Browser: Specify directory where PLLR CSV files will be saved
④ Start GOP Extraction Button: Execute PLLR computation and export results

sections.content: Key Features: Alignment dropdown dynamically loads based on selected dataset, outputs two CSV files (phonewise_proba.csv and framewise_proba.csv), settings accessible via gear icon (⚙️) for acoustic model configuration, and uses pypllrcomputer library under the hood.
Evaluate aligner performance against reference alignments. Status: 🚧 Not currently integrated (exists but not added to PipelineFormStack).

items: Ⓑ Evaluation Method Dropdown: Select evaluation strategy (Cross-Validation, Holdout Test, Custom)
Reference Path Browser: Specify ground-truth alignment directory
Predicted Path Browser: Specify predicted alignment directory to evaluate
Output Path Browser: Specify where evaluation results should be saved
Evaluate Aligner Button: Run evaluation script and generate metrics

sections.content: Note: This stacker is commented out in __init__.py and not currently accessible in the UI.
The PipelineFormStack container manages navigation between stackers:

Menu Item                    → Stacker Class         → Index
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Ⓐ  Train Aligners           → TrainingStacker       → 0
Ⓑ  Predict Alignments       → PredictionStacker     → 1
Ⓒ  Extract GOP Scoring      → PLLRStacker           → 2
(Ⓑ  Evaluate Aligner)        → EvaluationStacker    → (commented out)

items: Alignment dropdowns auto-populate based on dataset selection
Model dropdowns filter by selected engine
Empty states show 'No X registered' placeholders
All stackers have gear icon (⚙️) that opens engine-specific settings
Settings stored in JSON files per engine/tool combination
Uses GenericDialog with SettingsConfig framework
Status label shows: 'Ready' → 'Processing...' → '✓ Success' or '✗ Error'
Color-coded: Gray (ready), Orange (processing), Green (success), Red (error)
Uses WorkerThread for long-running operations to avoid UI blocking
Training: Blue (#4a90e2)
Prediction: Blue (#4a90e2)
GOP Extraction: Green (#27ae60)
Evaluation: Blue (#4a90e2)

sections.content:
Symbol           Typical Usage
─────────────────────────────────────
①②③④⑤⑥          Sequential workflow steps
Ⓐ Ⓑ Ⓒ           Major section identifiers (page titles)
⚙️               Settings/configuration access
✓ ✗             Success/error status indicators

installation.title: Installation Guide
installation.description: Detailed instructions for installing VoxKit on different platforms

sections.content: VoxKit supports macOS, Windows, and Linux. Choose your platform below for specific instructions.

items: Python 3.8 or higher
4GB RAM minimum (8GB recommended)
50MB free disk space
Audio input device (for real-time analysis)

sections.content:
# Using Homebrew
brew install voxkit

# Or using pip
pip3 install voxkit
Download the Windows installer from our downloads page and run it. Alternatively, use pip:

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install python3-voxkit

# Or using pip
pip3 install voxkit
If you encounter issues during installation, check that you have the latest version of pip:

configuration.title: Configuration Options
configuration.description: Customize VoxKit to match your workflow and requirements

sections.content: VoxKit can be configured through a configuration file or programmatically. This guide covers both approaches.
Create a voxkit.config.json file in your project root:
You can also configure VoxKit directly in your code:

from voxkit import Config, Analyzer

# Create custom configuration
config = Config(
    sample_rate=16000,
    channels=1,
    model='advanced'
)

# Initialize analyzer with config
analyzer = Analyzer(config=config)

items: sample_rate: Audio sampling rate (8000-48000 Hz)
channels: Number of audio channels (1 for mono, 2 for stereo)
bit_depth: Bit depth for audio processing (16, 24, or 32)
model: Analysis model to use (base, advanced, or custom)
output_format: Result format (json, csv, or xml)
verbose: Enable detailed logging (true/false)

api-reference.title: API Reference
api-reference.description: Complete reference for VoxKit's Python API

sections.content: VoxKit provides a comprehensive API for voice analysis. This reference covers the core classes and methods.
The main interface for analyzing audio data.

items: analyze(audio_path): Analyze a complete audio file
analyze_stream(stream): Analyze audio in real-time
set_config(config): Update analyzer configuration
get_features(): Extract audio features
export_results(format): Export analysis results

sections.content:
from voxkit import Analyzer

# Basic analysis
analyzer = Analyzer()
result = analyzer.analyze('speech.wav')

# Access results
print(f"Duration: {result['duration']} seconds")
print(f"Confidence: {result['confidence']}")

# Stream analysis
with open_audio_stream() as stream:
    for chunk_result in analyzer.analyze_stream(stream):
        print(chunk_result)

troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.description: Solutions to common issues and error messages

sections.content: This guide helps you resolve common issues you might encounter while using VoxKit.
If you see this error, VoxKit may not be properly installed in your Python environment.

# Solution: Reinstall VoxKit
pip uninstall voxkit
pip install voxkit

# Verify installation
python -c "import voxkit; print(voxkit.__version__)"
VoxKit supports WAV, MP3, FLAC, and OGG formats. If you're getting this error:

items: Check that your audio file has a supported extension
Verify the file is not corrupted
Convert the file to WAV format using an audio converter
Ensure the file has proper read permissions

sections.content: If analysis is running slowly, try these optimizations:

items: Use a lower sample rate (e.g., 16000 Hz instead of 44100 Hz)
Process shorter audio segments
Use the 'base' model instead of 'advanced'
Close other memory-intensive applications
Ensure you have sufficient RAM available

sections.content: If your issue persists, you can:

items: Check our GitHub issues for similar problems
Join our community Discord server
Email support at support@voxkit.dev
Review the detailed logs with verbose mode enabled

sections.content: Explore these practical examples to learn how to use VoxKit effectively.

from voxkit import Analyzer

analyzer = Analyzer()
result = analyzer.analyze('recording.wav')

print(f"Duration: {result['duration']}s")
print(f"Sample Rate: {result['sample_rate']} Hz")
print(f"Channels: {result['channels']}")

from voxkit import Analyzer
import os
import json

analyzer = Analyzer()
audio_dir = './audio_files'
results = []

for filename in os.listdir(audio_dir):
    if filename.endswith('.wav'):
        filepath = os.path.join(audio_dir, filename)
        result = analyzer.analyze(filepath)
        results.append({
            'file': filename,
            'result': result
        })

# Save results
with open('results.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)

from voxkit import Analyzer, FeatureExtractor

analyzer = Analyzer()
extractor = FeatureExtractor()

# Analyze audio
result = analyzer.analyze('speech.wav')

# Extract specific features
features = extractor.extract(result, features=[
    'pitch',
    'energy',
    'mfcc',
    'spectral_centroid'
])

print(f"Average Pitch: {features['pitch']['mean']} Hz")
print(f"Energy: {features['energy']['mean']} dB")
